quizzes.scenario: Counsel for the Defendant attempted to call a witness who was present in court pursuant to a subpoena. The Judge refused to allow the witness to testify, stating that he had already heard enough evidence from the Defendant.
options: The Judge has the power to limit the number of witnesses a party calls.
The refusal to allow a party to call witnesses is a breach of the duty to grant a fair hearing.
Only the prosecution can determine when enough evidence has been led.
The Judge is acting to prevent "undue descending into the arena."
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The court has a responsibility toward a lawyer to allow him to call his witnesses; failing to do so breaches the constitutional provision of "fair hearing" under Section 36 CFRN [1], [2].

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner who is a salaried Director of "XYZ Ltd" appeared fully robed in the High Court to represent the company in a contractual dispute. The Judge refused to grant him audience in that capacity.
options: The Judge's refusal is proper because a lawyer in salaried employment cannot appear as an advocate for his employer.
The Judge breached the lawyer's right of audience under Section 8 of the LPA.
A Director can always represent his own company if he is a lawyer.
The Judge should have allowed him to appear if the case was pro bono.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 8(1) and 8(3) of the RPC provide that a lawyer in salaried employment or a Director of a company shall not appear as an advocate in court for his employer [13], [14].

quizzes.scenario: In a complex commercial litigation, the Judge noticed that the Plaintiff's counsel was struggling to articulate a basic point of law regarding the Statute of Frauds. The Judge intervened to clarify the legal position to ensure the case was decided on its merits.
options: The Judge is unduly descending into the arena and should remain silent.
A Judge is to be impartial but is also not to sit unconcerned where counsel's incompetence is likely to cause injustice.
Intervention in counsel's argument is always a breach of fair hearing.
The Judge should cite the counsel for professional negligence instead of helping.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While a Judge must be impartial, he is not to sit unconcerned where counsel's incompetence is likely to cause injustice, as stated in Musa Omo Eleja v Bangudu [2].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer arrived at the High Court for a hearing and found the front row empty. Being a lawyer of 3 years post-call, he sat in the front row. The Judge ordered him to move to the back, stating the front row is reserved.
options: The Judge's order is a breach of the lawyer's right of audience.
The Judge is correct as the front row is reserved for SANs and Life Benchers.
Every legal practitioner is equal and can sit anywhere.
Junior lawyers have the right to sit in the front if they arrive first.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 26(2) of the RPC on equality is subject to the rules of precedence; the front row is reserved for SANs and Life Benchers as per Section 6 of the LPA [15], [16].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN) appeared alone to conduct a civil case in the High Court. The Judge refused to allow the trial to proceed until the SAN provided a junior counsel.
options: The Judge is wrong because a SAN can appear alone in any court.
The Judge is correct because a SAN shall not appear as counsel in any civil case before a superior court of record without a junior.
The rule against appearing alone only applies in the Supreme Court.
The Judge should have fined the SAN instead of stopping the trial.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 2(1) of the Senior Advocates of Nigeria (Privileges and Functions) Rules 1979 precludes a SAN from appearing in civil cases without a junior or another SAN [17], [18].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge discovered that the lawyer appearing before him was the same lawyer who had filed a petition against him to the National Judicial Council (NJC) in a previous matter. The Judge refused to hear the lawyer, citing "personal animosity."
options: The Judge is right to protect his dignity.
The Judge must still accord the lawyer a right of audience provided no legal disability exists.
The lawyer should have been cited for contempt immediately.
The Judge should have asked the client to change counsel.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The court has a responsibility to grant a fair hearing and right of audience to counsel regardless of personal feelings, as the duty is to the institution of justice [1], [5].

quizzes.scenario: During a hearing of a motion, the Judge noticed that the counsel was wearing a dark suit but had forgotten his wig and gown. The Judge refused to hear him and ordered him to leave the bar.
options: The Judge is correct as counsel must be properly robed in Superior Courts.
The Judge should have proceeded as the suit is a dark color.
Robing is only mandatory for the delivery of judgment.
Only the NBA can enforce robing rules, not the court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 45 of the RPC and court decorum rules provide that at the Superior Courts, counsel must appear robed in their wigs and gowns [19], [20].

quizzes.scenario: A High Court Judge was observed having a private lunch with the lead counsel for the Plaintiff in the middle of a contested trial. The defense counsel raised an objection.
options: The Judge is free to associate with lawyers outside the courtroom.
A Judge should be cautious and avoid personal relationships with lawyers that may affect the discharge of his duties impartially.
The defense counsel should have been invited to the lunch to balance the relationship.
This is only an issue if the Judge pays for the lunch.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 2(1) of the Code of Conduct for Judicial Officers provides that a Judge should avoid personal relationships with lawyers which may affect impartial discharge of duties [21], [12].

quizzes.scenario: An English-trained Barrister obtained a warrant from the Chief Justice of Nigeria to represent a client in a specific fraud case in Nigeria. At the hearing, the trial Judge refused to hear him, stating he is not on the Nigerian Roll of Legal Practitioners.
options: The Judge is correct because only those on the roll can practice in Nigeria.
The Judge is wrong because a person authorized by warrant under Section 2(2) of the LPA is entitled to practice for those specific proceedings.
The warrant only allows him to act as a solicitor, not an advocate.
He needs to be a Nigerian citizen to use the warrant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 2(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act allows the CJN to issue a warrant to an advocate from a similar legal system to practice for the purpose of particular proceedings [22], [23].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge presiding over a civil matter in the FCT High Court insisted on addressing a female legal practitioner as "My Learned Colleague" instead of the traditional address.
options: The Judge is correct because all lawyers are equal.
The correct address from a Judge to a legal practitioner is "My Learned Friend."
The Judge should have addressed her as "Counselor."
There is no rule governing how Judges address lawyers.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In court decorum, while lawyers address each other as "My Learned Friend," the Judge also uses this term, reflecting the "Ministers in the Temple of Justice" principle [7], [24].

quizzes.scenario: During a heated argument over the admissibility of a document, the Judge threatened to send the counsel to prison immediately without a hearing if he spoke one more word.
options: The Judge is exercising his summary power to punish contempt in facie curiae.
The Judge's threat is an act of intimidation and a breach of the duty to counsel.
Counsel has no right to argue once a Judge has made a preliminary remark.
The counsel should have been put in the dock first to show cause.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The duty of the court includes respect for counsel and avoiding intimidation. Summary contempt power must be used with caution and not for personal aggrandizement [25], [5].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge in a State High Court decided to conduct the entire trial in his private chambers because the courtroom's air conditioning was broken. Counsel for the Defendant objected, stating he wanted the trial in open court.
options: The Judge is correct to prioritize the comfort of the parties.
The court has a duty to conduct proceedings in public to ensure a fair hearing.
Trials in chambers are the same as trials in open court.
Only the Chief Judge can decide where a trial takes place.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 36 of the Constitution requires proceedings to be held in public. A Judge should not move to chambers without legal justification as it may affect the appearance of a fair trial [26], [5].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer who is a party in a civil suit (the Plaintiff) appeared in full robes to argue his own case. The Judge ordered him to de-robe before addressing the court.
options: The Judge is wrong because a lawyer can always robe when appearing in court.
The Judge is correct because a lawyer shall not wear his robes when conducting his own case as a party.
The lawyer can robe only if he is representing a co-plaintiff.
The Judge should have allowed him to robe but remain in the gallery.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 45(2)(b) and Rule 36(f) of the RPC provide that a lawyer shall not wear his robes when conducting his own case as a party to a legal proceeding in court [27], [28].

subtopics.subtopicName: Interviewing and Counseling Skills and Ethics

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Adewale, a legal practitioner, visits his high-profile client, Chief Emeka, at the Chief's private residence to take instructions for a new land matter because the Chief is "too busy" to visit the office. There is no physical infirmity or security threat involved. What is the ethical implication of Mr. Adewale's action?
options: It is professional as it is a high-profile client
It is professional as long as the Chief pays the appearance fee
It is unprofessional conduct as a lawyer should not take instructions at a client's house without special circumstances
It is professional since the Chief is a long-standing client
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 22 of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not call at a client's house or place of business for the purpose of giving advice or taking instructions, except in special circumstances or for some other urgent reason. "Busy schedule" does not constitute a special circumstance like infirmity or custody [1-3].

quizzes.scenario: During an initial interview with Madam Kofo regarding a breach of contract, Barrister Tunji fails to mention that the matter could be resolved through the Lagos Multi-Door Court House, preferring to file a writ of summons immediately. Has Tunji breached any ethical duty?
options: No, because litigation is the primary duty of a lawyer
Yes, he has a duty to inform the client of ADR options
No, because ADR is only for matrimonial causes
Yes, but only if the client asked for ADR
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 15(3)(d) of the RPC 2023, a lawyer shall not fail or neglect to inform his client of the option of alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanisms before resorting to or continuing litigation on behalf of his client [4-6].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Okafor informs his lawyer, Chidi, during a private session that he intends to burn down his competitor's warehouse next week to "level the playing field" in their pending lawsuit. Chidi decides to warn the competitor and the police. Is Chidi's action ethically permissible?
options: No, it violates the rule of lawyer-client privilege
Yes, disclosure is permitted to forestall the commission of a crime
No, unless the competitor is also Chidi's client
Yes, but only if Chidi withdraws from the case first
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 19(3)(c) of the RPC 2023 and Section 192(1)(a) of the Evidence Act provide exceptions to the rule of confidentiality, allowing a lawyer to reveal a client's confidence when it relates to the intention to commit a crime and disclosure is necessary to forestall it [7-9].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Amaka is interviewing a client who is narrating a long
options: complex story about a land dispute. Amaka allows the client to speak for 20 minutes without interruption
only nodding and maintaining eye contact to encourage the flow. Which skill is Amaka primarily employing? "
Active questioning
Passive listening
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Analysis stage

quizzes.scenario: In the middle of an interview, a client reveals that the opposing party is Barrister Dele's biological father. Dele realizes this creates a conflict but believes he can still win the case. What is Dele's immediate ethical duty?
options: Continue the case but don't tell the client to avoid anxiety
Disclose the relationship to the client at the time of the retainer or as soon as it is discovered
Only disclose if the father's lawyer finds out
Ignore it as family ties do not affect professional judgment
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 17(1) of the RPC 2023 @tipulates that a lawyer shall, at the time of the retainer, disclose to the client all the circumstances of his relations with the parties and any interest in the controversy which might influence the client in the selection of the lawyer [12-14].

quizzes.scenario: After a long interview, Barrister Segun concludes that his client's claim for defamation is completely barred by the Statute of Limitations and has no chance of success. He is worried the client will be angry if he says so. What should Segun do?
options: File the case anyway and hope the other side doesn't notice
Tell the client the case is strong to keep the retainer
Inform the client that the claim is hopeless
Advise the client to lie about the dates to bypass the limitation period
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 14(2)(e) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall inform his client where he considers the client's claim or defense to be hopeless [15-17].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer's secretary
options: while having drinks with friends
mentions the details of a high-profile divorce case being handled in the firm. Is the lawyer ethically responsible for this breach of confidence? "
No, the lawyer did not speak, the secretary did
Yes, the lawyer must ensure employees preserve client secrets
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Yes, but only if the lawyer was present when the secretary spoke

quizzes.scenario: During an interview, Barrister Aisha asks her client: "Did you sign the document on the 5th of May at the bank?" This question is an example of what?
options: An open-ended question
Leading question
Closed-ended question
Mirror question
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A closed-ended question is one that demands a specific response or a "yes/no" answer, often used to fill in gaps or clarify specific details [20, 21].

quizzes.scenario: A client instructs Barrister Obi to bribe a court official to ensure a case is assigned to a "favorable" judge. Obi explains this is illegal, but the client insists. What is Obi's required course of action?
options: Comply with the client's instructions as the client is the boss
Withdraw from the employment immediately
Bribe the official but keep it a secret
Only bribe if the amount is reasonable
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 15(2) of the RPC 2023 states that where a client insists on a course of conduct that is illegal or a breach of the law, the lawyer shall withdraw his employment [22-24].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Funmi is following the Avrom Sherr model of interviewing. She has finished listening to the client's narrative and is now asking follow-up questions to clarify ambiguities. Which stage is she in?
options: Listening stage
Questioning stage
Advising stage
Execution stage
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Avrom Sherr propounded three broad stages: Listening, Questioning, and Advising. The Questioning stage is where the lawyer fills in gaps and clarifies ambiguous statements [20, 25, 26].

quizzes.scenario: During the counseling stage, Barrister Oke identifies three options for his client: litigation, mediation, or doing nothing. The client chooses litigation despite Oke's preference for mediation. What should Oke do?
options: Refuse to represent the client
Force the client to choose mediation
Respect the client's final decision provided it is within the law
Sign a settlement agreement on the client's behalf anyway
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Counseling should be client-centered; the lawyer provides the options and legal implications, but the final decision rests with the client, provided the course of action is lawful [24, 27].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Jude takes notes verbatim during a 2-hour client interview, writing down every single word the client says. What is the professional critique of this approach?
options: It is the best way to ensure no facts are lost
Verbatim note-taking is discouraged as it interferes with active listening and eye contact
Verbatim notes are required by the RPC for all interviews
It is professional only if the client is paying by the hour
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The sources state that note-taking should be of important points and not verbatim, as a human being cannot effectively listen and write verbatim simultaneously, and it distracts from the interview process [28, 29].

quizzes.scenario: A client comes to Barrister Joy for an interview but cannot pay the consultation fee immediately. Joy decides to take the instructions for free as the client is her elderly aunt. Is this ethically permissible? "
options: No, undercutting is a professional misconduct
Yes, a lawyer may charge no fee for a relative or indigent person
No, every client must pay the scale fee
Yes, but only if she reports it to the NBA
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While Rule 52(1) of the RPC 2023 prohibits undercutting, it provides an exception that a reduced fee or no fee at all may be charged on the ground of special relationship or the indigence of a client [30, 31].

quizzes.scenario: During an interview, Barrister Kazeem realizes that his firm is already representing the person his new client intends to sue. What is his duty?
options: Take the new brief and assign it to a junior to avoid conflict
Represent both and try to settle the matter
Refuse the retainer due to conflict of interest
Charge both clients double to compensate for the risk
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 17(4) of the RPC 2023 states that a lawyer shall not accept a retainer if the exercise of his professional judgment will be affected by his own interests or if it conflicts with the interest of an existing client [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: A client tells Barrister Tunde during an interview that he committed a murder three years ago for which someone else was wrongly convicted. The client does not intend to turn himself in. Can Tunde reveal this to the authorities? "
options: Yes, because it is a serious crime
No, it is a past crime protected by lawyer-client privilege
Yes, but only with the Judge's permission
No, unless the client is currently being tried for that murder
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 19 of the RPC and Section 192 of the Evidence Act protect communications regarding past crimes. The exception for commission of a crime applies to future or ongoing crimes, not those already completed [7-9].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Chidi uses highly technical Latin terms like "consensus ad idem" and "caveat emptor" throughout an interview with a rural farmer who has little formal education. Which skill has Chidi failed to apply effectively?
options: Research skill
Advocacy skill
Verbal communication skill
Drafting skill
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Effective verbal communication requires using proper, positive, and simple common words, avoiding legal jargon or "legalese" that the client may not understand [9, 32].

quizzes.scenario: According to the "Law School Version" of the stages of a client interview, what are the five stages?
options: Listening, Questioning, Advising, Execution, Closing
Preparation, Beginning, Client telling story, Analysis, Conclusion
Starting, Gathering, Analyzing, Drafting, Ending
Welcome, Facts, Law, Fees, Goodbye
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Law School version uses the acronym PBCAC: Preparation for the interview, Beginning, Client telling the story, Analysis, and Conclusion [33].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Mike is interviewing a witness of the opposing party in a civil matter without the consent of the opposing counsel. He advises the witness not to talk to the other side anymore. Is this ethical?
options: It is ethical as it is a civil matter
It is ethical because he doesn't need the other lawyer's consent
It is unethical as he must not advise the witness to secrete themselves
It is unethical because he must always get consent to talk to any witness
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 25(1) of the RPC 2023 allows a lawyer to interview an opposing party's witness without consent, provided he does not hide the witness from the opposing party or advise the witness not to communicate with them [34, 35].

quizzes.scenario: During the analysis stage of an interview, Barrister Bola summarizes her impression of the client's story and asks the client if she has captured everything correctly. This is an example of what type of question?
options: Open-ended question
Hypothetical question
Mirror/Confirmatory question
Leading question
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Mirror or confirmatory questions are used by the lawyer to summarize their impression and confirm that they have correctly understood the information divulged by the client [27, 36].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Dare accepts a brief but, after the interview, discovers that the matter is in an area of law he has never practiced and knows nothing about. He decides to handle it alone to keep the entire fee. Is this ethical?
options: Yes, because a lawyer is a "jack of all trades"
No, he must associate with a competent lawyer unless the client objects
Yes, as long as he does some research
No, because he must only practice in one area
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 16(a) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not handle a legal matter which he knows or ought to know that he is not competent to handle without associating with a lawyer who is competent to handle it, unless the client objects [30, 37, 38].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Emeka, a legal practitioner, erects a large billboard in Ikeja with the caption: "Emeka & Co: We have won 95% of our cases in the last five years. Come to us for guaranteed victory."
options: This is proper as it provides accurate information to the public
This is improper as it includes a statement about the success rate of the lawyer's practice
This is proper because lawyers are now permitted to advertise under the 2023 Rules
This is only improper if Mr. Emeka is not a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 39(2)(d) of the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) 2023, a lawyer is prohibited from engaging in any advertising which includes statements about the quality of his work, the size of his practice, or his success rate [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Mr. Kunle, visits a local land registry to search for defects in the titles of various properties. After finding several irregularities, he contacts the property owners and offers to represent them in litigation against the government.
options: This is a proactive legal service and is professional
This is improper as it constitutes instigating litigation and searching registries for defects with a view to employment
This is proper if Mr. Kunle only charges a fixed fee
This is only improper if the property owners are already represented by counsel
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 47(1) and 47(2)(a) of the RPC 2023 provide that a lawyer shall not foment strife or instigate litigation, and specifically prohibits searching land registries for defects in title with a view to employment or litigation [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Bola enters into an agreement with a hospital ward attendant to pay him 10% of any professional fees earned from accident victims referred to her firm by the attendant.
options: This is a valid referral fee agreement
This is proper provided the client is informed of the arrangement
This is improper as it involves offering rewards to persons to influence legal work and engaging agents to follow up on accidents
This is only improper if the percentage exceeds 50%
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 47(2)(c) and (d) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from engaging agents to follow up on accidents or offering rewards to any person to influence legal work in favor of the lawyer [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Tunde, a legal practitioner, distributes handbills at a busy bus park in Lagos, listing his firm's address and claiming to be "The most affordable and efficient lawyer for all criminal matters."
options: This is proper under the general permission for advertising
This is improper solicitation of professional employment via handbills
This is proper if the information on the handbills is accurate
This is only improper if Mr. Tunde is not in full-time practice
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(3)(a) of the RPC 2023 explicitly prohibits a lawyer from soliciting professional employment, either directly or indirectly, through the use of handbills, circulars, or advertisements [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: A law firm, "Justice Seekers LLP," creates a website comparing their professional fees with three other prominent firms in the same locality, claiming their services are superior and cheaper.
options: This is proper competition in a free market
This is proper if the comparison is based on published NBA fee scales
This is improper as it makes comparisons with or criticizes other lawyers
This is proper provided the information is not misleading
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(2)(c) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not engage in any advertising or promotion which makes comparisons with or criticizes other lawyers or other professions [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: Following a major airplane crash, Barrister Chidi immediately sends letters to the grieving relatives of the victims, whom he does not know, offering his legal services to sue the airline for damages.
options: This is a humanitarian act and is professional
This is improper as it constitutes seeking out claimants in respect of personal injuries to gain employment
This is proper if Barrister Chidi has expertise in aviation law
This is only improper if Barrister Chidi charges a contingent fee
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 47(2)(b) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from seeking out claimants in respect of personal injuries or any other cause of action with a view to being employed by the prospective client [4, 5].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Yusuf, a legal practitioner, publishes a brief biographical profile in a reputable Law Directory, including his name, address, schools attended, and his membership in the Nigerian Bar Association.
options: This is a form of prohibited advertising
This is improper solicitation
This is a permitted form of advertisement
This is only permitted for Senior Advocates of Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(4) of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer to publish informative data about himself, including name, address, and academic qualifications, in a reputable law list or law directory [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Mrs. Ada, appears on a national television program to discuss general principles of landlord and tenant law. During the show, she offers to provide free private consultations to viewers who call her personal number displayed on the screen to discuss their individual rights.
options: This is proper as it promotes legal education
This is improper as she accepted employment to advise on individual rights during a public program
This is proper provided she does not charge for the initial consultation
This is only improper if she is robed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 46(1) of the RPC 2023 allows a lawyer to participate in media programs to give information on the law, but strictly prohibits accepting employment from such programs to advise on inquiries regarding individual rights [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Segun, a sole practitioner, uses a business card that describes him as "The Legal Lion: Specialist in Winning Hopeless Cases."
options: This is proper as it showcases his specialty
This is proper provided he has won at least one "hopeless" case
This is improper as it is self-aggrandizing and misleading
This is only improper if the card is distributed to non-clients
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(2)(a) and (d) and 39(3)(e) of the RPC 2023 prohibit advertising that is misleading, includes statements about success rates, or constitutes self-aggrandizement [1, 9, 13].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Mr. James, relocates his office to a new address. He sends a dignified notice of this change to his existing clients and also places a small advertisement of the change in a local newspaper.
options: This is improper solicitation of new clients
This is a permitted form of announcement
This is only proper if he obtains permission from the NBA Branch Chairman
This is improper because newspaper advertisements are strictly prohibited
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 43 of the RPC 2023 provides that upon a change of address or telephone number, a lawyer may send a notice to his clients and may also insert an advertisement of such change in a newspaper or journal [12, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Musa, an expert in Intellectual Property law, sends a brief and dignified announcement of his availability to act as a consultant to other legal practitioners in his city.
options: This is improper as he is soliciting from colleagues
This is a permitted form of announcement under the Rules
This is only proper if he is a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
This is improper as it should only be sent to the NBA National Secretariat
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 44 of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer available to act as an associate or consultant to other lawyers to send a brief and dignified announcement of his availability to lawyers in his locality [12, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Okoro, a lawyer, puts up a sign outside his office building. The sign is 15 feet tall, features flashing neon lights, and includes a large photograph of himself in full legal robes.
options: This is proper as it clearly identifies the office location
This is improper as the sign is not of reasonable size and sober design
This is proper provided he paid the local government signage tax
This is only improper if the photograph was taken before he was called to bar
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 41 of the RPC 2023 requires that signs or notices outside a lawyer's office must be of "reasonable size and sober design," and Rule 39(3)(c) prohibits procuring one's photograph to be published in connection with one's practice [13, 15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: While walking through a shopping mall, Barrister Jane notices a man arguing with a store manager. She approaches the man, hands him her visiting card, and tells him, "You have a great case for breach of contract. Call me and I will sue them for you."
options: This is professional zeal to protect a citizen's rights
This is improper solicitation by personal communication
This is proper if she offers to handle the case pro bono
This is only improper if the man is already represented by another lawyer
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(3)(a) and Rule 47(1) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from soliciting professional employment by personal communication or proffering advice to bring a lawsuit without being consulted [3, 7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner, Mr. Bitrus, writes a scholarly article for a law journal on the "Evolution of Maritime Law in Nigeria." At the end of the article, he lists his name and his professional qualification as "LL.B, B.L, Maritime Law Consultant."
options: This is improper advertising of his specialty
This is proper and permitted under the Rules
This is only proper if the journal is owned by the NBA
This is improper because he should only list his name
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 42 of the RPC 2023 provides that where a lawyer writes an article for publication giving information on the law, he may add his professional qualification after his name [12, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Effiong is representing a high-profile politician in an ongoing corruption trial. During a break in the proceedings, he holds a press conference on the court steps, claiming his client is "100% innocent" and that the judge's previous rulings in the case were "biased and legally illiterate."
options: This is a proper exercise of the right to free speech
This is improper as it involves making extra-judicial statements likely to interfere with a fair trial and brings the profession into disrepute
This is proper if it is done to counter negative media reports about the client
This is only improper if the press conference was paid for by the client
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 33 and Rule 39(2)(b) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from making extra-judicial statements likely to prejudice a fair trial or diminish public confidence in the legal profession [2, 17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Sani, who is also a licensed real estate agent, runs a joint advertisement for his "Legal and Property Management Services," offering a discount on legal fees for any client who buys a house through his agency.
options: This is proper business diversification
This is improper as it involves practicing another profession simultaneously without GCB authority and is used to improperly attract business
This is proper provided the legal fees do not fall below the 2023 Remuneration Order scales
This is only improper if he is not a Senior Advocate of Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 7(1) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from practicing another profession simultaneously with the law without authority from the General Council of the Bar, especially where it is designed to improperly attract business [22, 24].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Miss Toyin, pays for a "Sponsored Post" on Instagram that features a video of her dancing in her wig and gown with a caption: "I am the baddest lawyer in town. My fees are the lowest, and I never lose. DM for your legal needs!"
options: This is proper modern marketing
This is improper solicitation and self-aggrandizement which brings the profession into disrepute
This is proper as long as the "baddest lawyer" claim is subjective opinion
This is only improper if she is not actually the "baddest lawyer"
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(2)(b), (d) and 39(3)(e) of the RPC 2023 prohibit advertising that brings the profession into disrepute, includes claims about success, or involves self-aggrandizement [1, 2, 9, 13].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister John agrees to pay a police officer a fixed sum for every suspect the officer allows him to speak with at the police station for the purpose of securing a brief.
options: This is a valid administrative facilitation fee
This is proper if the suspect is actually in need of a lawyer
This is improper solicitation through touts and influencers
This is only improper if the suspect is eventually convicted
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(3)(a) and Rule 47(2)(d) of the RPC 2023 prohibit soliciting through touts and offering rewards to persons who, by reason of their employment, can influence legal work [5-8].

quizzes.scenario: A law firm, "Global Law Chambers," prints its name, address, and the academic qualifications of its partners on its official envelopes and note-papers in a dignified font.
options: This is a prohibited form of advertising
This is proper and permitted under the Rules
This is only proper if the firm has at least 10 partners
This is improper because only the name of the firm is allowed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 40 of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer may cause to be printed on his note-papers, envelopes, and visiting cards his name, address, and academic and professional qualifications [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: Colleagues and Profession ""
options: Mr. Ade
a legal practitioner
wins a high-profile case and immediately posts a sponsored ad on Instagram stating his firm has a 99% success rate in commercial litigation to attract more clients. ""
It is a permitted form of advertisement under Rule 39
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: it is improper because statements on success rates are prohibited

quizzes.scenario: A prosecutor, Mr. Idowu, realizes that a witness for the defense is telling the truth which confirms the innocence of the accused, but he chooses to suppress this information to ensure a conviction for the state.
options: His primary duty is to procure a conviction for the State
He is exercising his discretion as an advocate
He has breached his primary duty to see that justice is done
He should only disclose the information if the defense counsel makes a formal request
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 37(6) of the RPC 2023 provides that the primary duty of a lawyer engaged in public prosecution is not to convict but to see that justice is done, and the suppression of facts establishing innocence is unethical. [7], [8], [9]

quizzes.scenario: During a civil trial, Lawyer Z finds a very recent Supreme Court decision that is directly against his client's case. He decides not to mention it, hoping the other side hasn't seen it.
options: He is protecting his client's interest as a loyal advocate
He has a duty to be candid and must disclose the authority to the court
It is the sole duty of the opposing counsel to find unfavorable authorities
He should only disclose the authority if the Judge specifically asks
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 32(1) and Rule 37 of the RPC 2023 require a lawyer to be candid and fair as an officer of the court, which includes a duty to disclose decided cases that are against his client's position. [17], [18]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Chidi is instructed by his client to bribe a court clerk to "lose" the file of a damaging witness so that the trial is delayed indefinitely.
options: Chidi must follow his client's instructions to win the case
Chidi must refuse and represent the client within the bounds of the law
Chidi should only do it if the client provides a written indemnity
Chidi should stay neutral and allow the client to handle the clerk
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 15(2) and (3)(a) of the RPC 2023 state that a lawyer must keep strictly within the law notwithstanding contrary instructions and shall not give service involving corruption of public officers. [24], [25], [26], [27]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Tunde, a lawyer in full-time employment as a director in a Federal Ministry, maintains his private law firm, "Tunde & Associates," and acts as the Managing Partner.
options: It is a legitimate way to manage his private affairs
It is a breach of the Code of Conduct for Public Officers
It is allowed if he does not personally appear in court
It is permitted if he specializes in an area not related to his ministry
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The 5th Schedule of the 1999 Constitution prohibits a public officer employed on a full-time basis from engaging in the management of any private business, profession, or trade except farming. [31], [32], [33]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer Greg, representing a high-profile defendant in a pending fraud case, holds a press conference to claim the Judge is "manifestly biased" and the trial is a "sham."
options: This is his right to free speech on behalf of his client
This is a breach of trial publicity rules and potential contempt of court
It is allowed if the Judge actually appears biased
This is permitted if the comments are made outside the court premises
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 33 of the RPC 2023 prohibits extra-judicial statements likely to prejudice a fair trial, and Rule 31(2) requires complaints against judicial officers to be made to appropriate authorities, not the press. [36], [37], [38]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer Sani is in court and hears the court orderly read out an entry in the record of proceedings that is clearly wrong but favors Sani's client. Sani stays quiet to protect the advantage.
options: He is fulfilling his duty of dedication to his client
He has a duty to the state and court to ensure justice and must correct the record
He should only correct it if the opposing counsel objects first
It is the Judge's sole responsibility to ensure the accuracy of the record
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 1 of the RPC 2023 states that a lawyer shall uphold the rule of law and promote the course of justice, which includes an obligation as an officer of the court to be candid and fair. [7], [43], [44], [45]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer A is aware that Mrs. X is already represented by Lawyer B in a land dispute, but Lawyer A proceeds to discuss a settlement offer directly with Mrs. X at her home without informing Lawyer B.
options: It is acceptable since it is for the purpose of settlement
It is a breach of duty to maintain good faith and fairness with a colleague
It is allowed if the client invited Lawyer A to the meeting
It is only allowed if the matter is non-contentious
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 27(5) of the RPC 2023, a lawyer shall not have any dealing with a person represented by another lawyer in the same matter without giving prior notice to the other lawyer. [3], [4]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), and Mr. Balogun, a Life Member of the Body of Benchers, appear in court for different mention matters. Who has precedence to mention his case first?
options: Chief Emeka (SAN) takes precedence as the higher rank
Mr. Balogun (Life Bencher) takes precedence over the SAN
Precedence is determined by whoever arrived in court first
The presiding Judge decides who speaks first based on the cause list
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 6(3) of the LPA and Rule 26(2) of the RPC 2023, Life members of the Body of Benchers take precedence over all other legal practitioners, including Senior Advocates of Nigeria (SAN). [10], [11]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer A accepts a brief from a client who has just dismissed Lawyer B. Lawyer A takes the file and continues the case without ensuring that Lawyer B's outstanding professional fees are settled.
options: Lawyer A has no professional obligation regarding Lawyer B's fees
Lawyer A must use his best endeavors to ensure the former lawyer is paid
The former lawyer must sue the client directly for his fees
Only the court can direct a new lawyer to pay the old one
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 29(3)(b) and Rule 27(4) of the RPC 2023 provide that a new lawyer accepting a brief shall use his best endeavors to ensure that all fees due to the former lawyer in the matter are paid. [3], [4], [16]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer P knows that Lawyer Q represents the defendant. Since Q is ten minutes late for court and hasn't filed an appearance, P immediately moves the court to dismiss the defendant's counter-claim without calling Q.
options: This is an efficient use of court time to favor his client
This amounts to sharp practice and a lack of fairness to a colleague
It is the defendant's fault for hiring a late lawyer
This is required by the duty of dedication to the client
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 27(2)(c) of the RPC 2023 states that when a lawyer knows the identity of the opposing counsel, he should not take advantage by causing a dismissal without first inquiring about the other lawyer's intention to proceed. [19], [20]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer X is asked by Lawyer Y to hold his brief in a complex murder trial. Lawyer Y hands over the bulky case file to Lawyer X only five minutes before the Judge sits.
options: Lawyer X should accept as a show of professional fellowship
Lawyer X should not accept if he cannot acquire an adequate grasp of the matter
Acceptance is mandatory because they are learned friends
Lawyer X must accept and then ask for an adjournment in open court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 27(3) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer shall not hand over a brief to another to hold, and the other shall not accept, unless it is handed over in reasonable time to acquire an adequate grasp of the matter. [3], [20]

quizzes.scenario: A client decides to dismiss Lawyer A and hire Lawyer B mid-trial. Lawyer B promptly files a notice in court but tells the client not to speak to or pay Lawyer A.
options: Lawyer B is being loyal to his new client
Lawyer B has a duty to promptly give notice to Lawyer A
Change of counsel only requires notification to the Registrar
Notice is only required if the court orders it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 29(3)(a) of the RPC 2023 requires that when a client changes lawyer, the new lawyer shall promptly give notice to the former lawyer. [39], [40], [16]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Benson, a legal practitioner, hires a group of young men to hang around police stations and the High Court premises to redirect litigants and detainees to his law office for a commission.
options: This is a creative way to ensure access to justice
This amounts to soliciting professional employment through touts
It is allowed if the touts are registered as paralegals
It is permitted if the commission is reasonable
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 39(3)(a) of the RPC 2023 prohibits a lawyer from soliciting professional employment either directly or indirectly through circulars, handbills, advertisement, or through touts. [1], [5], [6]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. John, a sole practitioner, decides to brand his law firm as "John, Obi & Co" to make his practice appear more established, even though Obi is not a lawyer.
options: It is a smart marketing move for a new practitioner
It is permitted if John plans to hire Obi as a clerk
It is a breach of rules against misleading names and unauthorized partnership
It is allowed as long as John is the only signatory to the account
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 5(4) of the RPC 2023 provides that a lawyer practicing alone shall not use a firm name with the addition of "and Co" or any other name capable of holding out his practice as a partnership. [12], [13]

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner, Miss Amina, allows her non-lawyer senior clerk to draft a Deed of Mortgage and sign Amina's name on it to help a client who is in a hurry.
options: Miss Amina has aided the unauthorized practice of law
This is efficient delegation of administrative tasks
It is allowed if Amina orally authorized the signature
It is allowed if the clerk has a Diploma in Law
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 3(1) and (2) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from aiding a non-lawyer in the unauthorized practice of law or signing their name on a document prepared by a non-lawyer as if it were prepared by the lawyer. [14], [15]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Kayode, a legal practitioner, hears about a major gas explosion in a community and immediately goes there to distribute his business cards to the victims to encourage them to sue.
options: He is providing necessary legal awareness to the community
This is prohibited as instigating litigation and seeking out claimants
It is a permitted form of advertisement for his specialization
It is allowed if the victims are indigent and need pro bono help
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 47(1) and (2)(b) of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from fomenting strife or instigating litigation and specifically from seeking out claimants in respect of personal injuries to gain employment. [21], [22], [23]

quizzes.scenario: A Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), Chief Williams, is personally instructed to appear in a Magistrate Court to argue a motion for bail for his childhood friend.
options: He is demonstrating the dignity of the rank by helping a friend
A SAN is generally prohibited from appearing in an inferior court
He is allowed if it is a criminal matter and he is appearing alone
He must appear with a junior or another SAN regardless of the court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: It is a restriction under the rules that a SAN shall not appear before an inferior court, which includes the Magistrate Court. [28], [29], [30]

quizzes.scenario: Lawyer Bola writes a series of educational law articles for a legal journal and adds her LL.M and "Barrister-at-Law" titles after her name.
options: This is improper self-promotion and advertising
This is a permitted form of displaying her professional qualifications
She should only include her name to avoid aggrandizement
She must remove "Barrister-at-Law" as it is a qualification, not a title
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 42 of the RPC 2023 permits a lawyer who writes a book or an article for publication to add his professional qualification after his name. [34], [35]

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Mr. Silas, agrees to give 20% of his legal fees to a local community leader who referred a lucrative corporate client to his firm.
options: It is a fair referral commission
It is a breach of the rule against sharing fees with a non-lawyer
It is allowed if the client is aware of the arrangement
It is permitted because it promotes the lawyer's business
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 3(1)(c) and Rule 53 of the RPC 2023 prohibit a lawyer from sharing his legal fees with a non-lawyer, as this aids in the unauthorized practice of law. [14], [41], [42]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Yusuf, a legal practitioner, is representing a client in a civil appeal. He finds a recent Court of Appeal decision that is directly against his client's position, but decides not to cite it because the opposing counsel is a young lawyer who has not cited it. What is the ethical implication?
options: Yusuf has acted correctly by protecting his client's interest as a loyal advocate.
Yusuf has a duty to be candid and must disclose the authority to the court.
Yusuf should only disclose it if the Judge specifically asks.
Yusuf should withdraw from the case to avoid misleading the court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 32(1) and Rule 37 of the RPC 2023 require a lawyer to be candid and fair as an officer of the court, which includes a duty to disclose decided cases that are against his client's position. [1], [2]

quizzes.scenario: During a trial, a lawyer, Mr. Ade, notices that the court clerk has made an error in recording the previous day's proceedings that is highly favorable to his client. What is Mr. Ade's duty?
options: He must remain silent and protect his client's advantage.
He has a duty to the state and court to ensure justice and must correct the record.
He should only correct it if the opposing counsel objects.
He should withdraw from the case immediately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 1 of the RPC 2023 states that a lawyer shall uphold the rule of law and promote the course of justice, which includes an obligation as an officer of the court to be candid and fair. [5, 6, 7]

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner, Mr. Effiong, is representing a plaintiff in a land suit. Halfway through the trial, Effiong realizes that he was a witness to the original survey agreement and his testimony is essential to prove the case. What is the proper course of action?
options: He should continue as counsel and also testify as a witness.
He should withdraw from the case and let another lawyer handle the trial while he testifies as a witness.
He should refuse to testify to protect the attorney-client relationship.
He should ask the judge to decide based on his oral arguments without his testimony.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 20 of the RPC provides that a lawyer should not accept employment if he knows he or a lawyer in his firm may be a witness; if he discovers this during trial, he should withdraw from the case [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial, Barrister Ibrahim finds a recent Supreme Court decision that completely destroys his client's case. The opposing counsel is a junior lawyer who is unaware of this decision. What is Ibrahim's duty?
options: He must remain silent and hope the other side doesn't find it.
He must disclose the authority to the court, even though it is against his case.
He should withdraw from the case immediately.
He should tell the opposing counsel about it in private but not the judge.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 32 of the RPC (and Rule 37) requires a lawyer to be candid and fair; he must not fail to cite a decided case that is against him which has not been cited by the opposing counsel [1

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner, Mr. Adams, is assigned a pro bono case by the Legal Aid Council. Adams decides not to prepare for the case or attend the trial since he is not being paid. What rule has he breached?
options: Rule 24 on the Cab Rank Rule
Rule 52 on undercutting
Rule 14 on dedication and Rule 16 on competence
Rule 7 on engaging in business
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 14 and 16, a lawyer must devote expertise and energy to the service of his client; Section 9(1) of the LPA makes a lawyer liable for negligence regardless of whether services are provided for a fee or pro bono [8, 18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A client instructs his lawyer, Barrister Dele, to prepare a deed of assignment for a land transaction. The client explicitly tells Dele to "backdate" the document by two years to avoid a new tax law. Dele complies. What is the ethical implication?
options: Dele has acted correctly by following his client's instructions.
Dele has breached his duty to represent the client within the bounds of the law.
Dele is protected by attorney-client privilege.
Dele has only committed a minor clerical error, not misconduct.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 15(1) and (2) of the RPC state that a lawyer's first duty is to uphold the law and he must keep strictly within the law notwithstanding any contrary instruction from the client [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner, Mrs. Idowu, is representing a client in a civil suit. She forgets the trial date and fails to attend court, leading to the case being struck out. When the client sues her for negligence, she points to a clause in their retainer agreement that says, "the lawyer shall not be liable for any mistakes made during the case." Is this clause valid?
options: Yes, parties are free to contract as they wish.
No, any provision purporting to exclude or limit a lawyer's liability for negligence in a contract is void.
Yes, but only if the client signed it before a Notary Public.
No, unless the NBA President approved the waiver.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 9(1) of the Legal Practitioners Act (LPA) provides that a person shall not be immune from liability for damages attributable to his negligence, and any contract provision purporting to exclude this liability is void [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Benson retains Barrister Amina for a civil litigation matter. After two hearings, Benson informs Amina that he is changing his lawyer to Barrister Kunle. Amina refuses to hand over the case file until her outstanding fees are fully paid. Is she within her rights?
options: No, she must hand over everything to the new lawyer immediately.
Yes, a lawyer has a lien on the papers or documents of his client in respect of unpaid fees.
No, she can only withhold the client's original title deeds but not the correspondence.
Yes, but only if she gets a court order to withhold the files.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 29(3)(c) of the RPC, the former lawyer is entitled to a lien on the papers or documents of his client in respect of unpaid fees [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Dele is retained by a client in a property matter. While the matter is ongoing, Dele discovers that the client is using his law firm to launder money from an illegal drug business. What must Dele do to comply with current Nigerian law and ethics?
options: He must keep the information secret due to attorney-client privilege.
He must withdraw his services and report the suspicious activities to the appropriate authorities like the EFCC/NFIU.
He should ask for a 10% cut of the laundered money as a "risk fee."
He should only tell his local NBA Chairman.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Money Laundering (Prevention and Prohibition) Act 2022 and Rule 57 of the RPC 2023, lawyers have an obligation to report suspicious transactions to the appropriate authorities, and privilege does not cover transactions in furtherance of an unlawful act [15, 16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Tolu collects 15 million Naira as a judgment debt on behalf of his client. Since he is currently renovating his own house and needs urgent cash, he deposits the money into his personal savings account, intending to pay the client back after his next big fee comes in. What duty has he breached?
options: Duty of competence
Duty of dedication
Duty to deal with client's property honestly and separately
Duty to accept brief
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 23(2) of the RPC provides that a lawyer who collects money for his client shall not mix such money with his own and must report and account for it promptly [20, 21].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Emeka receives a draft of ?5,000,000 from a judgment debtor in respect of a client's debt recovery case. He wants to open accounts to manage these funds. According to the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules, which accounts must he open?
options: A personal current account and a personal savings account
A Client's Account and an Office Account
A joint account with the client
A fixed deposit account in the client's name
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules, every lawyer in private practice must maintain a Client's Account for client money and an Office/Trustee Account for firm money [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: What type of money is defined as ""Client's money"" under the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules?
options: Money belonging to the lawyer's family
Money held or received by a legal practitioner on account of a person for whom he is acting as a client
Money earned as professional fees after the completion of work
Money paid by the NBA as a welfare benefit
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: "Client's money" is money held or received by a legal practitioner on account of a person for whom he is acting as a client, excluding money paid to him as a retainer or professional fees [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following may be paid into a Client's Account?
options: Only money belonging to the legal practitioner
Only client's money and trust money
Only loans from a bank
Only personal savings of the firm's partners
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rules 3 and 4 of the Accounts Rules, only client's money, trust money, and money necessary to open or maintain the account may be paid into the Client's Account [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Musa is preparing to make a withdrawal from his Client's Account. For which of the following purposes can he legally withdraw money from the Client's Account?
options: To pay his personal electricity bill
To pay money due to a client or on the client's direction
To buy a new car for his law firm's associate
To pay for his child's school fees
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 7 of the Accounts Rules, money may be withdrawn from a client's account for payments to or on behalf of the client, or for payment of a debt due to the practitioner from the client [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: In which of the following banks can a legal practitioner open a Client's Account in Nigeria?
options: Any commercial bank licensed by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN)
Any microfinance bank in the local government
Any foreign bank without a branch in Nigeria
Only the Central Bank of Nigeria itself
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A Client's Account must be opened in a bank licensed as a commercial bank by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: What is the main purpose of keeping a Client's Account separate from the Office Account?
options: To maximize interest earnings for the law firm
To prevent the commingling of client funds with the lawyer's personal or business funds
To avoid paying taxes to the Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
To show the bank that the lawyer has many clients
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The fundamental purpose of keeping a separate Client's Account is to prevent the commingling of client's funds with the lawyer's personal or business funds, ensuring client money is protected [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: If a client disputes the amount of professional fees a lawyer wants to withdraw from the Client's Account for completed work, what must the lawyer do?
options: Withdraw the money anyway and let the client sue
Leave the disputed portion in the Client's Account until the dispute is resolved
Close the entire account and open a new one
Pay the disputed money to the NBA Chairman
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where there is a dispute regarding the lawyer's fees, the disputed portion must remain in the Client's Account and cannot be withdrawn until the dispute is resolved or taxed [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Who is responsible for auditing the accounts of a legal practitioner under the Accounts Rules?
options: The Nigerian Bar Association President
A qualified and independent Chartered Accountant
The Auditor General of the Federation
The bank manager of the commercial bank
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The accounts of a legal practitioner must be audited annually by a qualified and independent Chartered Accountant to ensure compliance with the Rules [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: What is the consequence if a legal practitioner fails to comply with the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules?
options: He will be fined ?100 by the local government
He is liable to disciplinary action for professional misconduct before the LPDC
His commercial bank account will be automatically closed by the CBN
He will be banned from traveling abroad
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Non-compliance with the Accounts Rules constitutes professional misconduct and makes the legal practitioner liable to disciplinary action before the LPDC [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following is NOT ""Client's money""?
options: Money received for a land purchase on behalf of a client
Money paid as a security for court costs
Money paid to a lawyer as an advance for court filing fees
Money paid as an agreed and fully earned professional fee
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Agreed and fully earned professional fees belong to the lawyer and are not classified as client's money; they should be paid into the Office Account [3, 19].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Kazeem is auditing his firm's accounts. He notices that he used ?200,000 from the Client's Account to temporarily pay the firm's office rent, but fully replaced the money two weeks later before the client found out. What is the status of Kazeem's action?
options: It is proper since the money was fully replaced and no harm was done
It is a serious professional misconduct and constitute breach of trust
It is proper if Kazeem records the replacement as a loan
It is proper if the client is a close relative of Kazeem
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Temporary use of client funds for personal or business purposes, even if replaced, is a breach of the Legal Practitioners Accounts Rules and constitutes professional misconduct [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: How long is a legal practitioner required to preserve his books of accounts
options: ledgers
and records of bank transactions under the Accounts Rules?
At least one year
At least three years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Indefinitely

quizzes.scenario: In a situation where a legal practitioner acts as a sole trustee for a family estate, what type of bank account must he open to manage the estate's funds?
options: A personal joint savings account with his spouse
A Trustee/Trust Account separate from his Client's and Office Accounts
A standard Client's Account
A cash deposit box at the corporate office
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A legal practitioner acting as a sole trustee or co-trustee must maintain a separate Trust/Trustee Account under the Accounts Rules [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: What is the primary ledger that a legal practitioner must maintain for every individual client?
options: A general journal ledger
A Client's Ledger showing receipts and payments for each client individually
A cash book showing only total daily cash receipts
A personal diary ledger
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A practitioner must maintain a Client's Ledger that clearly and individually shows all transactions, receipts, and payments for each client [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: If a client requests a statement of his accounts showing how the funds deposited with the lawyer were utilized, within what time frame must the lawyer provide it?
options: Within one year of the request
Promptly or within a reasonable time upon request
Only after the entire litigation or transaction has concluded
Only if the client pays a statement fee
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the rules of professional conduct and accounts rules, a lawyer must promptly account for and report on client's property and funds upon request [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following is a valid method for drawing money from a Client's Account?
options: A cash withdrawal over the counter by the firm's clerk
A crossed cheque or bank transfer directly to the beneficiary
An oral instruction to the bank manager
A signed blank check given to the client
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Withdrawals from a Client's Account should be made via crossed cheques or direct bank transfers to ensure trackability and prevent unauthorized cash withdrawals [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: What should a legal practitioner do if he receives a single cheque that contains both client's money and the practitioner's earned professional fees?
options: Deposit the entire cheque into his Office Account first
Deposit the entire cheque into the Client's Account first and then transfer the practitioner's portion promptly
Split the cheque physically into two parts
Ask the client to write two separate cheques before taking any action
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Accounts Rules, a mixed cheque must be deposited into the Client's Account first, and the practitioner's portion must be transferred out promptly once determined [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following books is NOT mandatory for a law firm to maintain under the Accounts Rules?
options: A Cash Book
A Client's Ledger
A Journal or Transfer Book
A visitor register book
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While Cash Books, Client Ledgers, and Journals are statutory requirements under the Accounts Rules, a visitor register book is not [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: Under what circumstance can a commercial bank freeze or debit a Client's Account to settle a personal loan owed to the bank by the legal practitioner?
options: Whenever the legal practitioner defaults on his loan
Only with the oral consent of the bank manager
Never; a bank has no right of set-off or lien on a Client's Account for the lawyer's personal debts
Only if the client is also a customer of the bank
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A bank has no right of set-off, charge, or lien on a Client's Account in respect of any personal liabilities or loans of the legal practitioner [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: Who has the authority to inspect the books of accounts of a legal practitioner to verify compliance with the Accounts Rules?
options: The President of the Customary Court
The General Council of the Bar or the Disciplinary Committee
The local police inspector
The tax officer from the local government
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The General Council of the Bar or the LPDC has the authority to direct the inspection of a legal practitioner's books of accounts to ensure compliance [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Okafor wants to hire Barrister Zainab to handle his divorce suit. He asks her to charge her fees based on a percentage of the property she recovers for him during the settlement. Zainab agrees. Is this arrangement ethically permissible?
options: Yes
contingent fees are allowed in all civil cases in Nigeria.
No
a legal practitioner shall not enter into an agreement for a contingent fee in any matrimonial cause.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: provided the agreement is in writing and registered with the NBA.

quizzes.scenario: What is the primary statutory instrument that regulates the charging of fees by legal practitioners for non-contentious business in Nigeria?
options: The Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) 2023
The Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order (LPRO) 2023
The Magistrates' Courts Rules
The Evidence Act 2011
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order (LPRO) 2023 is the primary statutory instrument that regulates fees for non-contentious businesses like land agreements, probate, and corporate drafting [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Kunle prepares a Deed of Assignment for a property worth ?50,000,000. He wants to know the appropriate scale of fees to charge. Under the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order, how are scales of fees categorized?
options: Scale I
Scale II
and Scale III
Scale A
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: and Scale C

quizzes.scenario: A client agrees to pay Barrister Tunde a fee that is 30% lower than the minimum fee prescribed under Scale I of the Remuneration Order. Tunde accepts the brief because he is desperate for work. What is this practice called?
options: Under-charging
Undercutting
Pro bono service
Fee-splitting
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Undercutting is the practice of charging fees lower than the minimum prescribed scale in the Remuneration Order, and it constitutes professional misconduct under Rule 52 of the RPC [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: When is a legal practitioner legally entitled to sue his client for recovery of his professional fees? "
options: Immediately after the work is completed
After the expiration of one month from the date of delivery of a signed Bill of Charges to the client
Only after obtaining a judgment from the Magistrate Court
Whenever the client refuses to pay an oral demand
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 16 of the LPA provides that a legal practitioner can only sue for his fees after the expiration of one month from the date on which a signed Bill of Charges was delivered to the client [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: What essential document must a legal practitioner deliver to his client before he can commence a lawsuit to recover unpaid fees?
options: A Writ of Summons
A Letter of Demand
A signed Bill of Charges
An affidavit of urgency
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 16(1) of the LPA, a legal practitioner must serve his client with a signed Bill of Charges before he can commence any action in court to recover his fees [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: What does ""taxation of bills"" mean in the context of legal fees in Nigeria?
options: The payment of income tax on legal fees earned
The process where a court official (the Taxing Master) reviews and determines the reasonableness of a lawyer's bill of charges
The deduction of tax at source by the client
A special tax levy on high-profile law firms
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: "Taxation" of bills is the formal process where a court-appointed Taxing Master reviews and determines the reasonableness of a legal practitioner's bill of charges [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following is NOT a permissible method of charging fees for contentious business in Nigeria?
options: A fixed fee / Lump sum retainer
An hourly rate
A contingent fee in a criminal defense case
A retainer fee paid periodically
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A contingent fee in a criminal defense case is strictly prohibited under Rule 50(2) of the RPC and is therefore not permissible [2, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Kemi agrees to represent a client in a debt recovery suit. They agree in writing that Kemi's fee will be 15% of whatever amount is successfully recovered from the debtor. Is this agreement valid?
options: Yes, this is a valid contingent fee agreement for a civil contentious matter.
No, contingent fees are strictly illegal in Nigeria.
Yes, but only if the client pays a 5% registration fee to the NBA.
No, unless the debtor also agrees to pay the fee.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 50(1) of the RPC permits contingent fees in civil contentious matters, provided the agreement is in writing, reasonable, and not in matrimonial or criminal cases [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: What is a ""Retainer"" in the context of legal practice?
options: A device used to keep files organized
An agreement where a client pays a lawyer to secure his services over a period of time or for a specific series of matters
A fee paid to a junior lawyer for doing research
A penalty paid by a client for changing lawyers
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A retainer is an agreement where a client pays a legal practitioner to secure his services over a specified period or for a specific series of matters, ensuring the lawyer's availability [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A client is dissatisfied with the Bill of Charges delivered by his lawyer and believes the fees are grossly inflated. To whom should the client apply for the taxation of the bill?
options: The President of the Nigerian Bar Association
The Taxing Master (usually the Registrar of the High Court)
The Chairman of the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee
The Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: An application for taxation of a Bill of Charges is directed to the Taxing Master, who is typically the Registrar of the High Court of the State [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: Within what time frame can a client apply for the taxation of a Bill of Charges after receiving it without needing a court order?
options: Within one week
Within twelve months (one year) of delivery
Only within thirty days of delivery
Anytime within five years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 17 of the LPA provides that an application for taxation of a bill may be made within twelve months of delivery of the bill [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: What is the effect of a taxed Bill of Charges?
options: It is sent to the FIRS for tax computation
It has the effect of a judgment of the court and can be enforced directly
It is only a non-binding recommendation
It must be approved by the NBA NEC before enforcement
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Once a Bill of Charges is taxed and a certificate is issued, it has the force of a judgment of the court and can be enforced directly without a fresh trial [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Adams delivers a Bill of Charges to a client. The client pays the bill in full. Six months later, the client discovers that the fee charged was double the legal scale and wants to tax the bill. Can the bill still be taxed?
options: No, once a bill is paid, it can never be taxed.
Yes, the court can still order taxation under special circumstances even after the bill has been paid, provided the application is within 12 months.
Yes, but only if the lawyer agrees to the taxation.
No, unless the lawyer is first convicted of a crime.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 18 of the LPA, a bill can still be taxed after payment under special circumstances, provided the application is made within 12 months [4, 7].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following is a key element that must be included in a valid Bill of Charges under Section 16 of the LPA?
options: A detailed list of the lawyer's academic qualifications
The names of all the partners in the law firm
A clear statement of the items of work done and the specific charges for each item
The bank account number of the NBA
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A valid Bill of Charges must contain a clear statement of the specific items of work done and the corresponding fees, and must be signed by the practitioner or a partner [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: Under the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order 2023, what is the consequence if a lawyer charges a fee below the minimum scale for preparing a deed of transfer?
options: The deed of transfer becomes automatically invalid and void
The lawyer is guilty of the offence of undercutting and liable to disciplinary action
The bank will refuse to process the payment
The land registry will refuse to register the deed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Charging below the minimum scale constitutes the offence of undercutting under Rule 52 of the RPC, which is a professional misconduct [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: If a lawyer delivers a Bill of Charges and the client files an application for taxation, can the lawyer commence an action to recover the fees while the taxation is pending?
options: Yes, he can sue to recover the undisputed portion.
No, no action to recover the fees can be commenced or continued while the taxation of the bill is pending.
Yes, provided he gets permission from the local NBA Chairman.
No, unless the client is about to flee the country.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 16 of the LPA bars any action for recovery of fees while the taxation of the Bill of Charges is actively pending [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: What is a ""Contingent Fee"" agreement?
options: An agreement where the fee is payable only in the event of a successful outcome of the case
An agreement where the fee is paid in monthly installments
An agreement where the fee is determined by the size of the lawyer's office
An agreement where the client pays the lawyer in properties rather than cash
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A contingent fee agreement is one where the fee is payable only in the event of a successful outcome or recovery in the case [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: Under the Legal Practitioners Remuneration Order 2023, what are the geographical zones used to determine the applicable scale of charges?
options: Northern Zone and Southern Zone
Zone 1 (High density/commercial centers like Lagos and Abuja) and Zone 2 (Other states)
East
West
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: and South

quizzes.scenario: Who pays the cost of the taxation proceedings if the Taxing Master reduces the lawyer's bill by more than one-sixth of the original amount?
options: The client
The legal practitioner
The Nigerian Bar Association
The High Court registrar
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the standard rules of taxation, if the bill is reduced by more than one-sixth upon taxation, the legal practitioner pays the costs of the taxation proceedings [18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Kemi receives a draft of ?10,000,000 on behalf of her client, but refuses to hand it over or report it to the client. After three months of silence, the client wishes to file a complaint. To which body should the client submit the petition?
options: The National Executive Committee of the NBA
The Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee (LPDC)
The Body of Benchers
The Disciplinary Committee of the local NBA Branch
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Complaints against a legal practitioner for professional misconduct are submitted to the LPDC (or through the NBA which forwards it to the LPDC) for investigation and trial [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: A young lawyer is accused of "infamous conduct in a professional respect." Which of the following acts is most likely to be classified as infamous conduct by the LPDC?
options: Filing a motion for adjournment on medical grounds
Commingling client's funds with personal funds and refusing to account for them
